efficacy_animal_model: Crl/NU(NCr)-Foxn1nu mice
efficacy_description: The three-dose regimen of 1 MBq per dose not only significantly prolongs the survival of HER2-positive metastatic ovarian cancer models through targeted alpha therapy (core efficacy) but also maintains good body weight stability (low toxicity), making it the optimal monotherapy regimen. A higher dose (2 MBq per dose) did not improve efficacy; instead, it caused weight loss and no improvement in survival due to renal toxicity, suggesting a narrow therapeutic window for the drug. Strict dose control is therefore required to balance efficacy and safety.

efficacy_animal_model: Crl/NU(NCr)-Foxn1nu mice
efficacy_description: The combination of 213-Bi-DTPA-2Rs15d trastuzumab is the optimal regimen. It not only extends the median survival to more than 140 days but also almost completely eliminates tumor burden with good safety. The low-dose monotherapy (0.5 MBq per dose) is also effective, increasing the survival time by 51% compared with the control group, and is suitable for scenarios where combination therapy is intolerable or dose reduction is required.

efficacy_animal_model: HER2-expressing SKOV3 ovarian cancer xenograft mice
efficacy_description: This study compared the efficacy of the chelators CHX-A″-DTPA and H4octapa when conjugated to the therapeutic radiometal ⁹⁰Y. When both chelators were coupled with trastuzumab, high radiochemical yields were achieved within 15 minutes at room temperature. They exhibited excellent in vitro and in vivo stability, high immunoreactivity, and strong tumor uptake in mice bearing SKOV3 ovarian cancer xenografts. After 36 days of treatment, the two conjugates showed comparable tumor growth inhibition effects, making both suitable for ⁹⁰Y-related radioimmunotherapy applications.

efficacy_animal_model: PC12 tumor-bearing mice
efficacy_description: This study evaluated the efficacy of the α-emitter ²¹¹At-MABG in the treatment of pheochromocytoma. In vitro experiments showed that it dose-dependently inhibits PC12 cell survival and induces DNA damage. In in vivo experiments, it exerted a potent tumor-reducing effect on tumor-bearing mice, with doses of 0.56 MBq and above demonstrating significant efficacy. Additionally, it had mild side effects, and the mice's body weight was recoverable. Compared with conventional ¹³¹I-MIBG, it exhibits superior therapeutic efficacy, providing a potential effective therapeutic option for malignant pheochromocytoma.

efficacy_animal_model: B16F10 tumor-bearing mice
